planting.note: Plant pups (suckers) or tissue-culture plants in spring after danger of frost; in zone 9 plant March–May. Dwarf Orinoco is one of the most cold-hardy edible banana cultivars, surviving temperatures down to 20–22°F at the roots even when the above-ground pseudostem is killed to the ground — the rhizome survives and sends up new suckers in spring. In zone 9 the pseudostem typically survives mild winters intact, allowing it to reach the fruiting stage in the first or second season.

directions: Plant in full sun in rich, well-drained, moisture-retentive soil with high organic matter; Dwarf Orinoco is a compact, cold-hardy edible banana producing medium-sized, angular, somewhat starchy fruits that can be eaten fresh when fully ripe (developing a rich, sweet-tart flavor) or cooked as a plantain when green. The Orinoco type bananas (also called 'Horse Banana') are among the most cold-hardy fruiting bananas in cultivation, with Dwarf Orinoco selected for a shorter stature (6–10 feet) that is more manageable in home gardens and more wind-resistant than tall-growing types. In zone 9 Dwarf Orinoco regularly produces edible fruit clusters on pseudostems that survive mild winters without die-back, making it one of the most reliably productive edible banana choices for the Gulf South. Feed generously with high-potassium fertilizer during the growing season to support fruit development.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Banana Production in Louisiana
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cold-Hardy Banana Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Bananas for Texas Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant tissue-culture plants or rhizome pups in spring after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Gran Nain performs best in zone 9b and 10 where winters rarely damage the pseudostem. In zone 9a mulch heavily around the base in fall and provide frost protection for the pseudostem during hard freezes to preserve the harvest timeline.

directions: Plant in full sun in rich, well-drained, moisture-retentive soil with abundant organic matter; Gran Nain is the world's most commercially important banana — the standard Cavendish-type export banana sold under the Chiquita and Dole brands. The medium to large, curved, smooth-skinned, sweet, mild-flavored fruits are the archetype of the supermarket banana and are grown on multi-stem mats that require minimal specialized knowledge. Gran Nain grows 8–12 feet tall — moderately tall for a Cavendish type — and produces large, heavy bunches of 7–9 hands when given full sun, abundant water, and high nitrogen and potassium fertilization throughout the growing season. In zone 9 the long, hot growing season produces excellent banana development; the main challenge is protecting pseudostems from the occasional hard freeze. Zone 9 gardeners who can grow Gran Nain successfully typically achieve production equivalent to small commercial planting density.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cavendish Banana Cultivars
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Gran Nain Banana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Banana Variety Selection for the Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant pups or tissue-culture plants in spring after frost; in zone 9 plant March–May. Red Dacca is ornamentally striking — the dark red-maroon pseudostem, leaf undersides, and unripe fruit color make it one of the most visually distinctive edible bananas. In zone 9 protect from hard freezes; mulch heavily at the base in late fall to insulate the rhizome.

directions: Plant in full sun in rich, well-drained, moisture-retentive, fertile soil; Red Dacca (also called Red Banana, Claret Banana, or Pisang Raja Udang in Southeast Asia) is a distinctive red-skinned banana cultivar producing medium-sized fruits with deep maroon to reddish-purple skin when unripe that turns dark yellow-orange when fully ripe. The flesh is creamy white with a slightly firmer, sweeter, and more aromatic flavor than standard Cavendish bananas — often described as having hints of raspberry or tropical fruit notes beyond the typical banana flavor. The entire plant is ornamentally striking, with dark red-purple pseudostems, contrasting green leaf tops and red-purple leaf undersides, and the colorful fruit clusters adding exceptional visual interest. Red Dacca is widely grown as a dual-purpose ornamental and edible banana in zone 9-11 gardens and is popular in Southeast Asian and Caribbean cuisine both fresh and cooked. The fruits are ready to harvest when the skin color begins to lighten from deep red toward orange-red.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Red Banana Cultivars
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Ornamental and Edible Bananas
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Red Dacca Banana

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant April–June. Florida Cranberry roselle is a short-day flowering plant — it begins setting calyces as days shorten in September–October regardless of when it was planted, so earlier planting results in a larger, more productive plant at calyx-setting time. The large calyces ripen October–November in zone 9.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Florida Cranberry is a named roselle cultivar selected specifically for very large, fleshy, deeply colored calyces — the thick, star-shaped, burgundy-red sepals that encircle each seed pod after the flower drops and are the edible product of roselle cultivation. The deep cranberry-red calyces have an intensely tart, cranberry-like flavor with hibiscus floral notes, used to make a vivid crimson tea (agua de Jamaica), jam, syrup, sauce, and desserts. The exceptional calyx size and deep color of Florida Cranberry make it the standard named cultivar for calyx production in the Gulf South. In zone 9 the short-day flowering response means calyces set abundantly in October–November when temperatures are cooling — a natural alignment with the Thanksgiving and holiday season when cranberry-flavored products are in demand. Harvest calyces before the seed pod matures for best flavor; the calyces are easily separated from the seed capsule by hand.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Roselle Florida Cranberry Cultivar
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Roselle Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Roselle and Hibiscus Tea Production

planting.note: Plant corms or divisions in spring when soil has warmed to 65°F; in zone 9 plant March–May. Bun Long taro is a wetland-adapted variety that thrives in consistently moist or periodically flooded conditions — ideal for poorly drained garden spots, pond margins, and bog gardens. It can also be grown in well-watered containers. The corms are harvested 7–12 months after planting when the large leaves begin to yellow.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in rich, moist to wet soil — Bun Long thrives at pond margins and in consistently waterlogged conditions that would kill most vegetables; Bun Long is a Hawaiian-adapted wetland taro cultivar used as the primary taro for poi production in Hawaii, producing large starchy corms with a distinctive pale lavender-purple flesh that turns light gray-purple when cooked and pounded. The large corms (often 2–5 pounds each) are a primary poi taro precisely because of their excellent starch content and texture when cooked and pounded or processed. The leaves and stems are also edible when thoroughly cooked — the tender young leaves (luau leaves in Hawaiian cuisine) are used in laulau, stew, and other dishes. In zone 9 Bun Long taro is an outstanding productive crop for the long, hot, humid growing season and thrives in the wet spots and poorly drained areas of Gulf Coast gardens where other vegetables struggle. The handsome large leaves with their distinctive pale purple veins provide ornamental value as well.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Poi Taro Cultivars
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Taro Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Taro Cultivation in Florida
